10.2 Exam — 3 February 2023

Realize a 3-bit counter that counts towards larger numbers when a control line is set to 0 and towards smaller numbers when it is set to 1.


Solution. Similarly to the decimal counter (paragraph [sec:decimal-counter]) I will consider the smallest number (encoded by \(000\)) as immediately following the largest number (encoded by \(111\)), and in the same way the latter as immediately preceding the first.
\[
\begin{aligned}
        &A^{n+1}=\bar{A}^n\\
        &B^{n+1}=\left[\bar{A}B\bar{S}+ABS+\bar{A}\bar{B}S+A\bar{B}\bar{S}\right]^n\\
        &C^{n+1}=\left[\bar{A}BC+ACS+\bar{B}C\bar{S}+AB\bar{C}\bar{S}+\bar{A}\bar{B}\bar{C}S\right]^n
\end{aligned}
\]


Using bit-masking operations, invert the seventh bit (starting from the most significant one) of a 32-bit register \(R_0\).


Solution. To invert a bit using bit-masking it is necessary to perform a XOR with a mask composed only of 0s except for a 1 in the position of the bit. Since this is in the seventh position from the left, it will be located in the second hexadecimal digit of the mask:
\[
0010=0\text{x}2
\]
Since there are 32 bits, the hexadecimal digits will be a total of \(8\), all equal to \(0\). The operation, therefore, will be the following:
\[
R_0 \ \textit{XOR} \ 0\text{x}02000000
\]


Write a program for SAP-1 that executes and finally shows the result of the arithmetic operation \(23-126+78\).
\[
\begin{cases}
            23//16=1 \ &    ext{with remainder} \ \red{7}\\
            1//16=0 \ & ext{with remainder} \ \red{1}
        \end{cases}
        \Longrightarrow \ 23_{10}=0\text{x}17
\]
\[
\begin{cases}
            126//16=7 \ &   ext{with remainder} \ \red{14}\\
            7//16=0 \ & ext{with remainder} \ \red{7}
        \end{cases}
        \Longrightarrow \ 126_{10}=0\text{x}7E
\]
\[
\begin{cases}
            78//16=4 \ &    ext{with remainder} \ \red{14}\\
            4//16=0 \ & ext{with remainder} \ \red{4}
        \end{cases}
        \Longrightarrow \ 78_{10}=0\text{x}4D
\]

What operation does the following program for SAP-2 perform?
Suggest an alternative program that performs the same operation.


Solution. The program loads into the accumulator the content of the register \(R_{0\text{x}2100}\), performs the two’s complement of the number and loads the result into the next address \(R_{0\text{x}2101}\).
An alternative way to perform the same operation is to load the number \(0\) into the accumulator and then subtract the content of the register from it, obtaining the same number with the opposite sign, which is precisely the result of the two’s complement.


Write a program for SAP-3 that checks if a signed number loaded into the accumulator is positive or negative using the minimum possible number of clock cycles. The sign of a number in the accumulator is determined by its most significant bit, so we can extract this bit through an AND with the byte \(0\)x\(80=1000\hspace{0.8mm}0000\) and send the result to the output: if the number was positive the bit was 0 and we will get \(0\)x\(00\), if instead the number was negative the bit was 1 and we will get \(0\)x\(80\).

text:
10.3 Exam — 24 February 2023

Find the truth table of the flip-flop described by the following equation:
\[
D^{n+1}=\left[D\oplus(MN)\right]^n
\]
Also, realize this flip-flop starting from a D flip-flop.


Solution. Let’s make the XOR explicit:
\[
D^{n+1}=\left[D\left(\overbar{MN}\right)+\bar{D}(MN)\right]^n
\]
In this form it is simple to write everything in the form of a J-K flip-flop:
\[
\begin{cases}
            J=MN\\
            \bar{K}=\bar{MN}
        \end{cases}
        \Longrightarrow \ \ J=K=MN
\]
We have already seen in the first exercise related to the D flip-flop (paragraph [sec:flip-flop-d]) the construction of a J-K flip-flop through a D flip-flop, so we will just have to replicate the same scheme where at the inputs \(J\) and \(K\) is placed \(MN\):


Using bit-masking operations, check that the third bit (starting from the most significant) of a 32-bit register \(R_0\) is set.


Solution. The third bit from the left defines the first hexadecimal digit as follows:
\[
0010=0\text{x}2
\]
All the other seven nibbles to the right will be zero. For the check, an AND with this mask is sufficient:
\[
R_0 \ \textit{AND} \ 0\text{x}20000000
\]
If the result is \(0\) the bit is 0, if instead the mask is obtained again the bit is 1.


Find the decimal value of the following floating points (according to the IEEE 754 standard):


Solution. \[
\begin{aligned}
        &0\text{x}40600000\\
        &0\text{x}41D00000
\end{aligned}
\]
Let’s start with the first one:
\[
0\text{x}40600000=\red{0}\orange{100}\hspace{0.8mm}\orange{0000}\hspace{0.8mm}\orange{0}\green{110}\hspace{0.8mm}\green{0000}\hspace{0.8mm}\green{0000}\hspace{0.8mm}\green{0000}\hspace{0.8mm}\green{0000}\hspace{0.8mm}\green{0000}
\]
Let’s calculate E and M:
\[
E=2^7=128
        M=2^{-1}+2^{-2}=0.5+0.25=0.75
\]
The sign is given by 0, so the number is positive and equal to
\[
1.75\cdot2^{128-127}=1.75\cdot2=3.5
\]
Let’s do the same calculations for the second number:
\[
0\text{x}41D00000=\red{0}\orange{100}\hspace{0.8mm}\orange{0001}\hspace{0.8mm}\orange{1}\green{101}\hspace{0.8mm}\green{0000}\hspace{0.8mm}\green{0000}\hspace{0.8mm}\green{0000}\hspace{0.8mm}\green{0000}\hspace{0.8mm}\green{0000}
\]
\[
E=2^7+2^1+2^0=128+2+1=131
        M=2^{-1}+2^{-3}=0.5+0.125=0.625
\]
\[
1.625\cdot2^{131-127}=1.75\cdot2^4=1.75\cdot16=28
\]


If the clock frequency of a SAP-1 is \(5 \ MHz\), how long does it take to execute a program that calculates \(23-126+78\) and shows the result?


Solution. The program is the same as the previous exercise, so it will be enough to refer to the code above.
Let’s calculate the time of each timing state:
\[
T=\frac{1}{5\cdot10^6 \ Hz}=200 \ ns
\]
We remember that all the instructions of the SAP-1 require \(6\) timing states with the exception of the HLT which requires \(4\), so in total we will have:
\[
6\cdot4+4=28
\]
From which we find the total time:
\[
T_{tot}=28\cdot200 \ ns=5600 \ ns=5.6 \ \mu s
\]


What check does the following program for SAP-3 perform? The program loads \(0\)x\(FF\) into the register B: \[B=1111\hspace{0.8mm}1111\] The content of the register \(R_{0\text{x}2800}\) is then loaded into the accumulator; let’s make this number explicit, indicated with the carry, with the most significant bit and with the least significant bit: \[A=R_{0\text{x}2800}=\red{C}\hspace{1.6mm}\green{M}XXX\hspace{0.8mm}XXX\orange{L}\] A rotation of the bits to the right is followed: \[A=\orange{L}\hspace{1.6mm}\red{C}\green{M}XX\hspace{0.8mm}XXXX\] - If \(\orange{L}=0\) the content of B is loaded into A and this will in turn be saved in the register \(R_{0\text{x}2810}\): \[R_{0\text{x}2810}=1111\hspace{0.8mm}1111\] - If \(\orange{L}=1\) the byte \(0\)x\(00\) is loaded into B, which then passes to the accumulator and finally to the register \(R_{0\text{x}2810}\): \[R_{0\text{x}2810}=0000\hspace{0.8mm}0000\] In summary: the program saves in \(R_{0\text{x}2810}\) a byte composed only of 1s if the least significant bit in \(R_{0\text{x}2800}\) is 0, while if the latter is 1 a byte composed only of 0s is saved in the same address.

Write a program for SAP-3 that finds the absolute value of a given number. My idea is the following: if we load the number \(0\) into the accumulator and then add the number that interests us, the sign flag will be activated. This will allow us to perform conditional jumps: if it is positive, through JP, we will go directly to the halt since the number is already in absolute value; if instead it is negative we will perform the two’s complement, and therefore we will complement the number and add \(1\). If this number is contained in a register X it is possible to replace ADI with ADD X; if instead the number is already contained in the accumulator to activate the flag it is possible to simply execute ADD \(0\)x\(00\).

Using eight bits for the fractional part, write in fixed point representation the decimal number \(34.2349\).


Solution. The integer part will be given by
\[
34=32+2=2^5+2^1=100010
\]
Let’s calculate the fractional part:
\[
\begin{aligned}
        0.2349\cdot2=0.4698 \ & ext{which has integer part} \ \red{0}\\
        0.4698\cdot2=0.9396 \ & ext{which has integer part} \ \red{0}\\
        0.9396\cdot2=1.8792 \ & ext{which has integer part} \ \red{1}\\
        0.8792\cdot2=1.7584 \ & ext{which has integer part} \ \red{1}\\
        0.7584\cdot2=1.5168 \ & ext{which has integer part} \ \red{1}\\
        0.5168\cdot2=1.0336 \ & ext{which has integer part} \ \red{1}\\
        0.0336\cdot2=0.0672 \ & ext{which has integer part} \ \red{0}\\
        0.0672\cdot2=0.1344 \ & ext{which has integer part} \ \red{0}\\
\end{aligned}
\]
It is not clear to me whether it is intentional or not, but with eight bits we only get an approximation of the requested number:
\[
34.2349_{10}\simeq100010.00111100
\]


If the clock frequency of a SAP-1 is \(2 \ MHz\), how long does it take to execute a program that multiplies a number by two and shows the result?


Solution. To multiply a number by two, just add it to itself.
Time of each timing state:

T=\frac{1}{2\cdot10^6 \ Hz}=500 \ ns
\]
Total number of timing states:

6\cdot3+4=22
\]
Total time:
\[
T_{tot}=22\cdot500=\num{11000} \ ns=11 \ \mu s
\]


Write a program for SAP-2 that swaps the two nibbles of the data contained in the address \(0\)$ 2001$ using the minimum possible number of clock cycles. I can’t think of any faster programs than the one that, trivially, rotates (left or right as it may be) all the bits four times. Obviously it is possible to replace RAR with RAL obtaining the same result.

What does the following program for SAP-3 do? First, the extended register HL is loaded with the data \(0\)x\(2501\): \[HL=0\text{x}2501\] The content of the memory indicated by HL is copied into C and A: \[A=C=R_{0\text{x}2501}\] The extended register HL is incremented, thus pointing to the next memory address; the content of the latter is subtracted from the content in the accumulator, which is the same content as the previous address: \[A=R_{0\text{x}2501}-R_{0\text{x}2502}\] The sign flag is therefore activated as follows: \[S=
        \begin{cases}
            0 \ \ \text{if} \ R_{0\text{x}2501}\geqslant R_{0\text{x}2502}\\
            1 \ \ \text{if} \ R_{0\text{x}2501}<R_{0\text{x}2502}
        \end{cases}\] The content of \(R_{0\text{x}2501}\) is copied again into the accumulator: \[A=R_{0\text{x}2501}\]
If the sign flag is null, the content of the accumulator, and therefore of \(R_{0\text{x}2501}\), is saved in the address \(R_{0\text{x}2503}\); if instead the sign flag is set, the content of the address to which the data pointer points, which is always \(R_{0\text{x}2502}\), is copied into A, and it is this that is saved in \(R_{0\text{x}2503}\).
In summary, the program saves in the address \(R_{0\text{x}2503}\) the largest content between that contained in the two previous addresses.
